frame_analyses.description: Trainee positioned at FLS trainer with both instruments visible. All 6 pegs loaded with colored rubber objects distributed across 6 pegs (yellow, blue, orange, green, red visible). Starting configuration shows pegs full.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Standard FLS peg transfer setup visible. LHTI branded trainer confirmed.

frame_analyses.description: Right-hand needle driver grasping first object from left yellow peg. Object positioned centrally on grasper tip. Left instrument hovering near center well.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Controlled grip on first object. Graspers appear well-coordinated.

frame_analyses.description: First object in transit midair between left yellow peg and center well. Both instruments visible, coordinated positioning.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Smooth object transfer, no tremor evident. Good instrument positioning.

frame_analyses.description: Object being placed in center well from right instrument. Multiple objects now visible in center dish (yellow, orange, green, red).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Precise placement into center target. Multiple objects already transferred successfully.

frame_analyses.description: Later phase of task showing center well with multiple colored objects (all 6 visible: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, cyan). Left instrument preparing to pick up object from center well for return phase.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Performing reverse transfer (center well back to pegs). Demonstrating hand dominance switch.

frame_analyses.description: Object in midair being transferred from center well back toward peripheral peg. Trainee actively moving objects back to original peg configuration.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Return phase progressing smoothly. Instrument coordination maintained.

frame_analyses.description: Placing object back onto blue peg from center well. Multiple objects still remaining in center well visible. Pegs showing restored objects.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Systematic return of objects to pegs. Task nearing completion.

frame_analyses.description: All 6 objects returned to peripheral pegs. Center well now empty or nearly empty. Final configuration shows original peg distribution restored (yellow pegs, blue pegs with objects returned). Timestamp shows 11:02:09 (elapsed time approximately 605-606 seconds from start at 10:56:08).
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Task completed. All objects successfully transferred and returned.

time_calculation_note: Video timestamps range from 10:56:08 (start, frame showing initial setup) to 11:02:09 (final frame showing completed task with all objects returned to pegs). Elapsed time = approximately 366 seconds (6 minutes 6 seconds).

penalties.description: Task completion time of 366 seconds exceeds expert and intermediate benchmarks (40-120s). Falls within novice range (120-300s) but at upper boundary.
penalties.note: Time penalty is handled via score formula, not separately deducted

task_specific_assessments.hand_dominance: right dominant confirmed by lead grasp patterns
task_specific_assessments.technical_notes: All 6 objects successfully transferred to center well and returned to original peg positions. No visible drops or objects remaining in field. Clean completion.

score_components.score_note: Negative score due to completion time exceeding maximum allowed time (300s). Task took 366 seconds, exceeding limit by 66 seconds.

confidence_rationale: Video timestamps clearly visible on all frames. Task progression through all phases observable. No ambiguity regarding object placement, transfers, or completion. Time calculation based on explicit video timestamps.
technique_summary: Trainee demonstrated competent bimanual coordination and systematic approach to peg transfer. All objects were successfully transferred and returned with no drops. However, total completion time of 366 seconds exceeds the 300-second maximum allowed time, resulting in task failure per FLS scoring rules. Performance falls in novice range for speed.
strengths: Zero dropped objects throughout entire task
Systematic approach: forward phase then reverse phase
All objects securely placed on pegs (no incomplete placements)
weaknesses: Transfer speed significantly slower than intermediate benchmark (85s for midair phase vs ~20-30s typical)
Object handling appears deliberate/cautious rather than efficient
Excessive time spent on individual object transfers
improvement_suggestions: Increase instrument speed and efficiency to reduce time per object transfer
Practice rapid object acquisition and placement sequencing
Improve hand-eye coordination to allow faster, more confident movements
Reduce hesitation during midair transfer phase — currently taking ~85s for what should be <30s
Focus on maintaining object control while increasing speed to reach novice-to-intermediate transition (120-180s range)
